items.title: Typical age of presentation
items.contentHtml:
Typical age: 4-8 years

  • Perthes disease (avascular necrosis of the developing femoral head) classically presents between 4 and 8 years, with a peak at 5-6 years.
  • In children under 4 years of age the prognosis is better due to greater potential for remodeling; In those over 8 years of age, the risk of residual deformity and early osteoarthritis is significantly higher.
  • Presentation outside this age range requires ruling out other causes of avascular necrosis: developmental dysplasia of the treated hip, hemoglobinopathies (sickle cell anemia), use of corticosteroids or Gaucher disease.

items.title: Male predominance
items.contentHtml:
Male sex most affected

  • Perthes disease predominantly affects male sex in a ratio of 4:1 to 5:1 with respect to female sex.
  • In males the age of onset is usually slightly older, but the general prognosis is similar when adjusted for age and extent of necrosis.
  • Affected girls tend to present more severe forms and a worse prognosis, probably due to more advanced skeletal maturation at the same age, which reduces remodeling time.

items.title: painless limp
items.contentHtml:
Clinical presentation: painless lameness

  • The most common initial symptom is painless lameness (or with minimal pain referred to the knee or thigh), which delays specialist consultation.
  • The pain, when it appears, is usually mild, mechanical in nature, and is exacerbated by physical activity; rest relieves it.
  • It is key to ask about referred pain to the ipsilateral knee (a sign of referred hip pain) so as not to confuse it with knee pathology.
  • The absence of intense pain or fever helps in the differential diagnosis with septic arthritis of the hip (urgency) and transient synovitis of the hip.

items.foco: Limitation of abduction and internal rotation
items.title: Limitation of abduction and internal rotation
items.contentHtml:
Limitation of abduction and internal rotation

  • Physical examination reveals limitation of abduction and internal rotation of the affected hip, with preserved or increased external rotation and flexion as compensation.
  • Reflex muscle spasm (adductor and psoas) may mimic a fixed contracture; Sedation or anesthesia allows the real limitation to be evaluated.
  • Loss of internal rotation is the most sensitive and earliest sign; an asymmetric internal rotation <20° with respect to the healthy side is suspicious.
  • Limited abduction (<30°) indicates more advanced involvement and risk of femoral head deformity.

items.title: Positive Galeazzi sign
items.contentHtml:
Positive Galeazzi sign

  • The Galeazzi sign (or Allis test) is positive when, with the patient in the supine position, hips and knees flexed at 90°, one knee is lower than the contralateral one, indicating relative shortening of the femur or hip dislocation.
  • In Perthes disease, the Galeazzi sign can be positive due to collapse of the femoral head or lateral subluxation, but is not specific; It also appears in developmental dysplasia of the hip and in advanced necrosis.
  • Its presence requires ruling out other causes of lower limb length discrepancy.

items.title: Catterall classification
items.contentHtml:
Catterall's Classification

  • The Catterall's classification (1971) evaluates the extent of necrosis in the femoral head in anteroposterior and Lowenstein (frog) radiographs.
  • Group I: involvement <25% of the epiphyseal nucleus, without collapse.
  • Group II: involvement of the 25-50%, with central sequestration and minimal collapse.
  • Group III: 50-75% involvement, with collapse of the lateral portion and medial preservation.
  • Group IV: involvement of the entire epiphysis (>75%), with complete collapse and loss of height.
  • Groups III and IV are considered to have a poor prognosis and usually require surgical treatment.

items.title: Herring classification (lateral pillar)
items.contentHtml:
Herring classification (lateral pillar)

  • The Herring classification (or lateral pillar classification) is based on the height of the lateral portion of the femoral epiphysis on anteroposterior radiograph in the fragmentation phase.
  • Group A: intact lateral pillar, without loss of height (excellent prognosis).
  • Group B: lateral pillar with height loss <50% (intermediate prognosis).
  • Group C: lateral pillar with height loss >50% (poor prognosis).
  • It is the most reproducible classification and with the best prognostic correlation; predicts the final sphericity of the femoral head and the risk of osteoarthritis.

items.foco: Initial phase: necrosis and bone condensation
items.title: Initial phase: necrosis and bone condensation
items.contentHtml:
Initial phase: necrosis and bone condensation

  • The initial phase (necrosis) lasts weeks to months; histologically there is death of osteocytes and bone marrow, but the initial radiograph may be normal.
  • The first radiological sign is bone condensation (increase in density) of the epiphyseal nucleus due to trabecular collapse and compression, visible between 2 and 4 weeks after the onset of symptoms.
  • The bone scan with technetium-99m shows an area of hypouptake (cold defect) in the femoral head, while MRI is the early gold standard: hypointensity in T1 and T2 in the epiphysis.
  • Plain radiography at this stage can show a widened joint space and a small lateral subluxation of the femoral head.

items.title: Fragmentation and collapse phase
items.contentHtml:
Fragmentation and collapse phase

  • In the fragmentation phase (2-6 months after onset) the femoral head is destructured, radiolucent areas and bone fragments appear separated by granulation tissue.
  • The collapse of the epiphysis occurs when the mechanical load exceeds the resistance of the necrotic bone, producing flattening of the femoral head (coxa flat).
  • The loss of height of the lateral crura in this phase is the main determinant of prognosis and Herring classification.
  • Lateral subluxation of the femoral head (>2 mm with respect to the acetabular rim) is a sign of poor prognosis and usually requires surgical containment.

items.foco: Treatment: containment of the femoral head
items.title: Treatment: containment of the femoral head
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: containment of the femoral head

  • The objective of treatment is containment of the femoral head within the acetabulum to maintain its sphericity during revascularization and remodeling.
  • In patients <6 years and with mild involvement (Catterall I-II or Herring A-B), conservative treatment with physiotherapy is indicated to maintain range of motion and avoid contractures, in addition to avoid loading (relative rest, not impact sports).
  • Containment can be achieved with abduction orthoses (e.g., Atlanta or Scottish Rite orthoses) that maintain the hip in abduction and internal rotation, but with low adherence and limited effectiveness.
  • In children >6 years or with moderate-severe involvement, surgical containment (femoral or pelvic osteotomy) is the first line to improve the prognosis.

items.foco: Indication for femoral or pelvic osteotomy
items.title: Indication for femoral or pelvic osteotomy
items.contentHtml:
Indication of femoral or pelvic osteotomy

  • The varizing femoral osteotomy (or containment) is indicated in children >6 years with involvement of Catterall III-IV or Herring C, or when there is lateral subluxation of the femoral head.
  • The pelvic osteotomy (Salter type or triple innominate) is preferred in cases with coxa magna or when the femoral osteotomy does not achieve sufficient coverage; also in children over 8 years of age.
  • The surgical window of opportunity is during the early fragmentation phase, before irreversible collapse; late surgery (healing phase) does not improve sphericity.
  • Goal: achieve an acetabular coverage rate >80% and center the femoral head in the acetabulum to allow remodeling.

items.foco: Prognosis according to age at onset and involvement
items.title: Prognosis according to age at onset and involvement
items.contentHtml:
Prognosis according to age at onset and involvement

  • The prognosis of Perthes disease depends mainly on the age at onset and the extent of involvement (Catterall and Herring classifications).
  • Children with onset <6 years and involvement <50% (Catterall I-II, Herring A-B) have an excellent prognosis, with complete remodeling and minimal deformity residual.
  • Onset >8 years or involvement >50% (Catterall III-IV, Herring C) is associated with a worse prognosis: increased risk of coxa magna, coxa plana, length discrepancy and early osteoarthritis in adulthood.
  • The presence of head signs at risk (Gage's sign, lateral calcification, subluxation, V-shaped radiolucent zone) also worsens the prognosis.
